title: Resonant Raman scattering in bilayer 3R-MoS$_{2}$
authors: Chinmay K. Mohanty, Kacper Walczyk, Tomasz Woźniak, Chengcheng Jiang, Adam Babiński, Clement Faugeras, Zhaolong Chen, Maciej R. Molas
abstract: Raman scattering is a powerful spectroscopic technique widely employed to investigate light-matter interactions and lattice dynamics in two-dimensional materials. Here, we investigate the temperature-dependent resonant Raman response of bilayer 3R-MoS$_2$. The study combines multi-wavelength Raman spectroscopy, photoluminescence measurements, and density functional theory calculations to track the evolution of excitonic transitions and resonance conditions. We observe contributions from both zone-centre and finite-momentum phonons, a pronounced quenching of the Stokes intensity at low temperatures followed by saturation, the emergence of anti-Stokes scattering above 130~K, and a strong deviation of the effective phonon temperature from the lattice temperature induced by resonance effects. These results demonstrate that the Raman response is governed by the interplay between incoming and outgoing resonance processes, providing deeper insight into exciton-phonon coupling in van der Waals materials.
summary: This work uses resonant Raman spectroscopy to probe the temperature dependence of lattice dynamics in bilayer 3R-MoS2. By correlating changes in excitonic transitions with phonon scattering intensities, the authors demonstrate that the Raman response is dictated by resonant coupling rather than simple thermal equilibrium. This offers deep insight into exciton-phonon interactions in van der Waals materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the temperature-dependent resonant Raman scattering response in bilayer 3R-MoS2, focusing on how excitonic resonances govern lattice dynamics.
main_result: The resonant Raman response is strongly governed by the interplay between incoming and outgoing exciton resonances, leading to deviations from simple thermal population models, particularly in the anti-Stokes/Stokes intensity ratio.
method: The research combines multi-wavelength Raman spectroscopy, photoluminescence measurements, and DFT calculations to analyze temperature evolution.
model_or_system: The physical system is bilayer 3R-MoS2, a transition metal dichalcogenide. The analysis models temperature dependence using the Varshni relation for excitonic energy shifts and the Boltzmann relation for intensity ratios.
key_observables: Raman intensity (Stokes/anti-Stokes), effective phonon temperature (T*), excitonic transition energies (XA and XB), and the temperature dependence of these quantities.
important_parameters: Temperature range (5–310 K), excitation laser energies (e.g., 1.96 eV), and the material structure (3R stacking).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed deviations in the intensity ratio are due to resonant scattering mechanisms rather than solely thermal population effects.
figures_summary: Figures show optical microscopy, temperature evolution of PL spectra, and the energy dependence of XA/XB excitons. Other figures illustrate phonon dispersion, temperature-dependent Stokes/anti-Stokes intensities, and the effective temperature derived from the intensity ratio.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the temperature dependence of excitons (Varshni fitting), then examines the resulting changes in the Stokes/anti-Stokes intensity ratio, culminating in the demonstration that resonance conditions dictate the observed phonon temperature.
why_interesting: The strong coupling between electronic excitations (excitons) and lattice vibrations (phonons) under resonant conditions provides a rich platform for studying non-equilibrium dynamics and energy transfer in low-dimensional quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Optical microscopy image of a CVD-grown bilayer 3R-MoS2 flake, indicated by a red arrow. (b) Raman scattering spectra of bilayer 3R-MoS2 measured at 5 K using excitation energies of 1.96, 2.21, and 2.41 eV with an excitation power of 300 µW. (c) Temperature evolution of PL spectra obtained under 2.41 eV laser excitation. The black dashed lines denote the energies of the XA and XB lines. The vertical red dashed line indicates the energy of the 1.96 eV excitation. (d) Emission energies of XA (yellow) and XB (blue) as a function of temperature. Solid black curves represent Varshni fits, and open yellow and blue symbols correspond to the energies of XA and XB relative to the...

FIG. 2. (a) Calculated phonon dispersion of 3R-stacked MoS2 BL. Raman-active modes at the Γ point of the BZ are indicated. (b) Temperature evolution of the Stokes and anti-Stokes branches in high-resolution RS spectra measured under resonant excitation (1.96 eV) at T = 5, 100, 210, and 310 K. The assigned phonon peaks, including Raman-active and acoustic modes, as well as their combinations arising from higher-order processes, are labelled.

FIG. 3. Temperature evolution of the resonant Raman response of bilayer 3R-MoS2. Panels (a)–(c) show the integrated intensities of the Stokes (red points) and anti-Stokes (orange points) components for the A2 1 +A3 1, A4 1 +A5 1, and E4 +E5 modes, respectively. Note that the vertical axis ranges are adjusted to the maximum intensity of each phonon mode. Panels (d)–(f) present the corresponding Stokes and anti-Stokes scattering energies together with the XA and XB excitonic transitions. The horizontal dashed line denotes the excitation energy EL = 1.96 eV, while the solid black curves represent the Varshni fits of the excitonic transitions.
